name: Burro Peak
short_description: BLM public land near Silver City offering easy rockhounding for quartz, chalcedony, and jasper specimens. Gentle slopes and scenic views at 6589 feet elevation.
description:
Burro Peak sits on New Mexico BLM land in Grant County, providing accessible rockhounding opportunities for collectors of all levels. The area is characterized by gentle slopes and moderate elevation, making it ideal for leisurely prospecting without strenuous hiking. Collectors can expect to find quartz specimens, chalcedony nodules, and jasper fragments scattered across exposed hillsides and washes.

The geology here reflects the Basin and Range province where Tertiary-age volcanic and hydrothermal activity deposited silica-rich minerals throughout the region. The gentle slopes expose weathered outcrops and erosional debris that concentrate collectible specimens on the surface, making this a popular spot for both beginners and experienced rockhounders. The area benefits from New Mexico's dry climate, which naturally exposes mineral specimens over time.

Expect typical high desert terrain with scattered desert vegetation. The 6589-foot elevation provides cooler temperatures than lower desert locations, making spring and fall the most comfortable collecting seasons. This is a personal-use only site under BLM regulations, so plan to keep your best finds within the 25-pound daily limit.
history: This region sits within the Basin and Range province where Tertiary-age rhyolitic volcanism and associated hydrothermal activity created widespread silica mineralization. Quartz and chalcedony deposits formed from silica-rich fluids that cooled and precipitated in fractures and cavities, creating the nodules and crystals found in surface exposures today.
tips: Search the exposed hillsides and wash channels where erosion naturally concentrates specimens - look for glassy white and cloudy quartz pieces and translucent chalcedony nodules. The best material is often found near the base of small outcrops where weathering has loosened specimens from the host rock. Bring a small classifier or magnifying glass to identify quality pieces before committing to the 25-pound daily limit. Early morning rockhounding allows you to work before afternoon heat and gives you time to explore multiple small areas across the gentle slopes.
terrain_notes: Elevation 6589 feet with gentle slopes throughout the area. Terrain is typical high desert with scattered low vegetation, exposed rocky hillsides, and seasonal washes. Walking surfaces are uneven but manageable, with rocky footing common. No major cliffs or hazardous drops. Typical hiking distance from parking is 0.25 to 1 mile depending on where you focus collection efforts.
best_season: October - April. The high desert location provides pleasant collecting conditions during cooler months. Winter days are mild at this elevation, while summer heat (frequently exceeding 85 degrees F) makes daytime collecting uncomfortable. Late fall and early spring offer the best combination of comfortable temperatures and good visibility.
written_directions: From Silver City, head east on US 180 for approximately 12 miles toward the Grant County area. Look for BLM access roads and pullouts on the north side of the highway in the vicinity of Burro Peak. The peak itself is a distinctive landmark visible from the road. Park at any suitable pullout and walk upslope toward exposed ridges and washes. A high-clearance vehicle is recommended for some access roads, though passenger vehicles can typically reach main pullouts.
road_conditions: US 180 is paved. Secondary BLM access roads vary from dirt to graded surfaces, passable for passenger vehicles in dry conditions. Some rougher pullouts may require high-clearance vehicles.
parking_notes: Multiple informal pullouts along BLM access roads near Burro Peak. No developed parking lot or facilities. Park safely off the road and ensure you do not block vehicle access.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. No use of explosives. Remove specimens only by hand tools. Leave the area as you found it.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No explosives or heavy machinery
Hand tools only
Leave no trace
nearest_services: Silver City, approximately 12 miles west. Full services including gas, food, water, and lodging available. Bayard (small community) approximately 8 miles east.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently sloping terrain accessible to most hikers. Uneven rocky footing common but no major elevation gains. Unpaved roads to parking areas. Sturdy footwear required. Not wheelchair accessible due to terrain and parking situation. Good option for families with children and those with moderate mobility.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at Burro Peak near Silver City?
faq.answer: Burro Peak offers excellent opportunities to find quartz, chalcedony, jasper, and agate specimens. Quartz is the most common find, appearing as clear to cloudy white crystals and nodules. Chalcedony nodules are also frequently discovered, particularly near exposed outcrops and wash areas. The silica-rich geology of this Basin and Range location naturally concentrates these minerals.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Burro Peak free or do I need to pay a fee?
faq.answer: Rockhounding at Burro Peak is completely free. The site is located on BLM public land, which allows recreational rockhounding at no cost. You do not need to purchase a permit, but you must follow BLM collection rules including the 25-pound daily limit per person.

faq.question: How many pounds of specimens can I collect per day at Burro Peak?
faq.answer: Under BLM FLPMA regulations, you may collect a maximum of 25 pounds of rock and mineral specimens per day. This is a standard public lands limit designed to preserve the area for all visitors. Plan your collection strategy accordingly to maximize the quality of your finds within this weight limit.

faq.question: When is the best season to go rockhounding at Burro Peak in New Mexico?
faq.answer: The best rockhounding season at Burro Peak runs from October through April. During these months, temperatures are mild and comfortable for outdoor collecting at the 6589-foot elevation. Avoid summer months (June through August) when daytime temperatures frequently exceed 85 degrees F, making extended collecting uncomfortable in the high desert environment.

faq.question: Is Burro Peak rockhounding location suitable for beginners and families with children?
faq.answer: Yes, Burro Peak is an excellent choice for beginning rockhounders and families. The gentle slopes, easy access, and abundant surface specimens make it ideal for collectors of all experience levels. Children as young as 6 years old can participate in collecting activities. The relatively mild terrain and short walking distances make it perfect for family outings.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring for rockhounding at Burro Peak?
faq.answer: Bring a small geologist's hammer, chisel, and sturdy gloves for safe collection. A small screen or classifier helps identify quality pieces before committing them to your 25-pound daily limit. Since the area is somewhat remote with spotty cell service, bring plenty of water, sunscreen, and a hat. Good hiking boots with ankle support are essential for the rocky, uneven terrain. Consider bringing a GPS unit or mapping app for navigation.
